rulings.Case Title: JOAN DOWER VS. OPEN TOP SIGHTSEEING USA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To File Exhibit Under Seal
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, July 10, 2019, Line 5, CROSS DEFENDANT TU DINH'S Motion To File Exhibit Under Seal Grant. No opposition filed, and the motion complies with California Rules of Court, Rules 2.550 and 2.551. The Court finds that there exists an overriding interest that overcomes the right of public access to the portions of the records to be sealed; the overriding interest supports sealing those records; a substantial probability exists that the overriding interest will be prejudiced if the record is not sealed; the proposed sealing is narrowly tailored; and no less restrictive means exist to achieve the overriding interest. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the cross-defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and states that the motion was not opposed, and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: JOAN DOWER VS. OPEN TOP SIGHTSEEING USA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion For Determination Of Good Faith Settlement
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, July 10, 2019, Line 5, CROSS DEFENDANT TU DINH'S Motion For Determination Of Good Faith Settlement Cross-Defendant Tu Quy Dinh's motion for determination of good faith settlement is granted. Mr. Dinh provides substantial evidence that the amount of the settlement is within the reasonable range of his proportional share of comparative liability for the plaintiff's injuries, and Defendants Open Top Sightseeing USA, Open Top Sightseeing San Francisco San Francisco, LLC, and John Frederich Kennedy fail to show that the settlement is out of the ballpark. (See Code Civ. Proc. § 877.6(d) [the party asserting the lack of good faith shall have the burden of proof on that issue]; Tech-Bilt, Inc. v. Woodward-Clyde & Assocs. (1985) 38 Cal.3d 488, 499.) Mr. Dinh has agreed to pay $50,000 to Plaintiff Joan Dower, the entire amount of his available liability insurance coverage, and his sealed declaration, which has been provided to the Court and to Defendants, shows that he has exceedingly modest assets and is effectively judgment proof. (Johns Decl., Ex. 7; see Dole Food Co., Inc. v. Superior Court (2015) 242 Cal.App.4th 894, 909 [relevant considerations include the financial conditions and insurance policy limits of settling defendants]; County of Los Angeles v. Guerrero (1989) 209 Cal.App.3d 1149, 1152 ["Notwithstanding the settlement's disproportionality, because Guerrero lacks other assets, substantial evidence supports the finding of good faith settlement"].) Defendants' speculation that Mr. Dinh "likely" has substantial undisclosed assets is unsupported by any admissible evidence and is insufficient to meet their burden. (See Cahill v. San Diego Gas & Electric Co. (2011) 194 Cal.App.4th 939, 968 [because party opposing motion "had the burden to prove the settlement was not made in good faith, it could have presented evidence on those issues to show Owners had the financial capacity or insurance coverage limits to pay a reasonable settlement amount"]; Brehm Communities v. Superior Court (2001) 88 Cal.App.4th 730, 736 ["the determination whether the settlement was in good faith must be based on competent, admissible evidence"].) Defendants' belated request for discovery into Mr. Dinh's financial condition is denied. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the settling cross-defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: NIKKI BAKER VS. FCA US LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For An Order Determining Restitution Pursuant To Defendants 998 Offer; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities; Declaration Of David C. Mcgaffey; Exhibits A-H; [Proposed] Order
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, July 10, 2019, Line 6, PLAINTIFF NIKKI BAKER'S Motion For An Order Determining Restitution Pursuant To Defendants 998 Offer Plaintiff Nikki Baker's motion for an order determining restitution pursuant to Defendant FCA US LLC's § 998 offer is granted in the amount of $28,003.83. Pursuant to Civil Code § 1793.2(d)(2)(C), the mileage offset is to be determined on the basis of "the number of miles traveled by the new motor vehicle prior to the time the buyer first delivered the vehicle to the manufacturer or distributor, or its authorized service and repair facility for correction of the problem that gave rise to the nonconformity." Here, that calculation is to be performed as of October 27, 2016, when Plaintiff reported problems with the transmission. (McGaffey Decl., Ex. BB.) Plaintiff's vehicle registration renewal fees are recoverable as incidental and consequential damages. (See Ruiz v. BMW of North America, LLC (C.D. Cal. May 7, 2018) 2018 WL 2106454, at *5-6.) Defendant's reliance on Kirzhner v. Mercedes-Benz USA, LLC (2017) 18 Cal.App.5th 453, review granted, No. S246444 (Feb. 21, 2018) is misplaced, as that decision has no binding or precedential effect while it is pending review in the California Supreme Court. (See Cal. R. Ct. 8.1115(e)(1).) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: FRANCESCA RASELLA VS. GURU SHARANAM, LLC A LIMITED LIABILITY CORP. DBA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Compel Inspection Of Real Property
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, July 10, 2019, Line 2, PLAINTIFF FRANCESCA RASELLA's Motion To Compel Inspection Of Real Property. Pro Tem Conor Moore, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff's motion to compel inspection of Sunnyside Hotel is denied. Allowing the inspection at this point appears unlikely to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. Plaintiff has testified at deposition that she does not remember the floor or the room on the floor that she entered on the day of the accident. It appears unlikely that another inspection of other rooms will lead to any relevant information if Plaintiff cannot testify that one of those rooms was the room she believed she was in when she fell. If Plaintiff can first determine what room she believes she was in when she fell, an inspection can be conducted of that room. The determination of the room must come first before the inspection can occur. Otherwise, Defendant is subject to a fishing expedition that will be unduly disruptive to the business without any reason to believe such an expedition will lead to admissible evidence. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sfjudgeprotem@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm, stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, then the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing if the tentative ruling is contested. If the tentative is not contested and there is no hearing on the motion, then Defendant must timely comply with Cal. Rule of Court 3.1312. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: RICHARD JONES VS. BLATTEIS REALTY CO., INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Hearing On Petition To Confirm Contractual Arbitration Award
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, July 10, 2019, Line 8, DEFENDANTS BLATTEIS REALTY CO., INC., JEREMY BLATTEIS' Notice Of Hearing On Petition To Confirm Contractual Arbitration Award Petitioners and Defendants Blatteis Realty Co., Inc. and Jeremy Blatteis' petition to confirm contractual arbitration award is granted. Plaintiff Richard Jones has failed to show that any of the limited statutory grounds for vacating an arbitration award under Code of Civil Procedure § 1286.2 is applicable. "Generally, courts cannot review arbitration awards for errors of fact or law, even when those errors appear on the face of the award or cause substantial injustice to the parties." (Richey v. AutoNation, Inc. (2015) 60 Cal.4th 909, 916; see also Moncharsh v. Heily & Blase (1992) 3 Cal.4th 1, 11.) Plaintiff has failed to show that the arbitrator exceeded his powers by failing to follow California law. (See Safari Associates v. Superior Court (2014) 231 Cal.App.4th 1400, 1403-04 ["California law is clear that 'arbitrators do not "exceed[ ] their powers" . . . merely by rendering an erroneous decision on a legal or factual issue, so long as the issue was within the scope of the controversy submitted to the arbitrators.'"]). Further, the 16-page corrected final award amply satisfies the requirement of the arbitration agreement that the arbitrator issue a written decision explaining his findings and conclusions of law. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The Court will sign the proposed judgment included with the moving papers. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: BRIAN SCHMITT VS. RUTH A MAGINNIS ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Memorandum Of Points And Authorities Iso Motion To Quash/Modify Subpoena; Requests For Attorneys Fees And Costs Of 3060.00 & Declaration Of Ugo O. Asobile, Esq
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, July 10, 2019, Line 4, PLAINTIFF BRIAN SCHMITT, KRISTA STRYKER's Motion To Quash/Modify Subpoena; Requests For Attorneys Fees And Costs Of 3060.00. Pro Tem Conor Moore, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff's motion to quash or modify subpoena to Bernstein & Andriulli is granted. Defendant did not conform the language of the subpoena to the court's previous order and instead sought to broaden the scope of the subpoena beyond what had already been ordered by this court. If Defendant wants to subpoena the requested records from Bernstein & Andriulli, Defendant must modify the language of the subpoena to conform to the previous order of this court. This motion should not have been necessary as the matter had already been decided. Accordingly, for misuse and abuse of the discovery process, Defendant and her counsel of record are jointly and severally sanctioned $1,000, payable to Plaintiffs no later than August 12, 2019. rulings.Case Title: ZAINALI "ZAIN" JAFFER VS. VUNGLE, INC.
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Arbitration And Dismiss Complaint Or, In The Alternative, Stay Proceedings
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, July 10, 2019, Line 12, DEFENDANT VUNGLE, INC.'S Motion To Compel Arbitration And Dismiss Complaint Or, In The Alternative, Stay Proceedings (tentative rulings part 1 of 2) Defendant Vungle, Inc.'s motion to compel arbitration of the claim asserted in Plaintiff Zainali "Zain" Jaffer's complaint is granted and the action is stayed pending conclusion of the arbitration proceedings. The court exercises its discretion to review evidence submitted with Vungle's reply brief. Plaintiff may respond at the hearing, including with submission of additional evidence. (Espejo v. Southern California Permanente Medical Group (2016) 246 Cal.App.4th 1047, 1056-1059; Alliant Ins. Services, Inc. v. Gaddy (2008) 159 Cal.App.4th 1292, 1308.) Vungle has met its burden to show by a preponderance of the evidence that Mr. Jaffer executed a valid arbitration agreement. (Espejo v. Southern California Permanente Medical Group (2016) 246 Cal.App.4th 1047, 1060.) Although Mr. Jaffer denies that the signature on the 20111 agreement is his, all of the evidence is to the contrary. Although the signature on the 2011 agreement does not appear identical to any of the exemplar signatures provided by him, neither do the exemplars appear identical when compared to each other; further, the 2012 Agreement bears two signatures by Mr. Jaffer and they are also not completely identical. (Lee Decl., Exhs. 1 and 2; Jaffer Decl. Exh. 1). The Lee declaration sufficiently authenticates the documents and also shows that Mr. Jaffer was aware of the two arbitration agreements and that they were shown to potential investors and that he not only never disputed their authenticity, but affirmed in a contractual agreement with investors that all employees and officers had signed such agreements. (Lee Decl. 9, 11, 15, Exhs. 3 and 4.) The fact that the agreement was not signed by a representative of the company does not show that it was not a valid agreement or that the company cannot enforce it against Mr. Jaffer. (See Serafin v. Balco Properties Ltd., LLC (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 165, 177 ["Just as with any written agreement signed by one party, an arbitration agreement can be specifically enforced against the signing party regardless of whether the party seeking enforcement has also signed, provided that the party seeking enforcement has performed or offered to do so. (Civ. Code, § 3388.)"]; see also Nguyen v. Applied Medical Resources Corp. (2016) 4 Cal.App.5th 232, 252-253.) The mutual promise to arbitrate is sufficient consideration for the agreement. (See Strotz v. Dean Witter Reynolds, Inc. (1990) 223 Cal.App.3d 208, 216, overruled on other grounds, Rosenthal v. Great Western Fin. Securities Corp. (1996) 14 Cal.4th 394 ["Where an agreement to arbitrate exists, the parties' mutual promises to forego a judicial determination and to arbitrate their disputes provide consideration for each other"].) (end of tentative ruling part 1 of 2) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: ZAINALI "ZAIN" JAFFER VS. VUNGLE, INC.
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Vungle, Inc. Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Arbitration And Dismiss Complaint Or, In The Alternative, Stay Proceedings (Tentative Rulings Part 2 Of 2)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, July 10, 2019, Line 12, Defendant Vungle, Inc. Motion To Compel Arbitration And Dismiss Complaint Or, In The Alternative, Stay Proceedings (Tentative Rulings Part 2 Of 2) Mr. Jaffer's single cause of action falls within the scope of the arbitration agreement. Because Mr. Jaffer has failed to show the existence of any substantive unconscionability, the agreement is enforceable. (See Pinnacle Museum Tower Assn. v. Pinnacle Market Development (US), LLC (2012) 55 Cal.4th 223, 247 ["Both procedural unconscionability and substantive unconscionability must be shown, but 'they need not be present in the same degree' and are evaluated on 'a sliding scale.'"].) Unlike in Higgins v. Superior Court (2006) 140 Cal.App.4th 1238, 1253, the agreement is not unilateral: it does not carve out any claims likely to be brought by the employer and it contains express language of mutuality binding the company as well as the employee. (Lee Decl. Exhs.1, 2; see also Roman v. Superior Court (2009) 172 Cal.App.4th 1462, 1473.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and states that the motion was not opposed, and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. (end of tentative ruling part 2 of 2) =(302/EPS)
